short.headline: India's population pyramid data lacks sex breakdown
short.dek: UN projections provide age distribution for both sexes combined up to age 49 in 2030. Sex-disaggregated data is needed for a true pyramid.
short.body: The available data from UN World Population Prospects gives India's population by 5-year age groups for both sexes combined. For 2000, figures are available for ages 0-24: the 0-4 group had 131.8 million, declining to 96.2 million for 20-24. For 2030, data extends to ages 0-49: the 0-4 group drops to 111.5 million, while the 25-29 group peaks at 128.7 million. This shows a narrowing base and a bulge in young adults. However, without male and female breakdowns, the classic population pyramid shape cannot be drawn. Also, older age groups (50+) are missing. More granular, sex-specific data is required to fully describe India's population pyramid.

article.title: India's population pyramid: what the data shows and what is missing
article.standfirst: UN population projections provide both-sex age distribution for ages 0-49 in 2030, but lack sex breakdown and older ages, limiting the depiction of a population pyramid.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## India's population pyramid: limited data

A population pyramid visualises the distribution of a population by age and sex. Typically, it shows males on one side and females on the other, providing insights into dependency ratios, workforce potential, and demographic trends. For India, the UN World Population Prospects (2024) provides estimates of population by 5-year age groups for both sexes combined, but does not offer sex-disaggregated data in the available extract. This restricts the ability to construct a true pyramid.

### What the data covers

The evidence includes median projections for India for two time points: 2000 and 2030. For 2000, five age groups are available: 0-4 (131.8 million), 5-9 (124.9 million), 10-14 (119.4 million), 15-19 (109.2 million), and 20-24 (96.2 million). For 2030, ten age groups are provided up to 45-49: 0-4 (111.5 million), 5-9 (112.4 million), 10-14 (117.5 million), 15-19 (122.7 million), 20-24 (125.1 million), 25-29 (128.7 million), 30-34 (124.8 million), 35-39 (118.9 million), 40-44 (111.9 million), and 45-49 (100.3 million). Note that these are projections, not census counts.

### Key observations

Comparing the youngest age group between 2000 and 2030 reveals a narrowing base: the 0-4 population declined from 131.8 million to 111.5 million. This suggests a reduction in fertility over time. In 2030, the largest cohort is the 25-29 age group (128.7 million), reflecting the large numbers of young adults entering prime working age. The population then gradually decreases with age, as expected.

### What is missing

For a complete population pyramid, we need data for males and females separately. Without this, we cannot assess gender imbalances, such as sex ratios at birth or differences in life expectancy. Additionally, age groups beyond 49 are absent, so the elderly population (50+) is not captured. The 2000 data only covers up to age 24, preventing a full comparison of the pyramid shape over time.

### Why this matters

Understanding the age-sex structure is critical for policy planning — from schooling and healthcare to pensions and housing. The current data gap means we can only talk about broad age trends, not the nuanced differences between men and women. To fully describe India's population pyramid, sex-disaggregated data for all age groups is essential.

sourceNotes: UN World Population Prospects 2024, median variant, for India.
Data covers both sexes combined for 5-year age groups.
caveats: Age groups beyond 49 years not included in provided data.
Only two time points (2000 and 2030) for youngest age groups.

caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
